segments.text: It is July the 7th, and I'm in the Connolly building and have the pleasure and honor of chatting with Daniel Hernandez about his Texas A&M student experiences.
Daniel, say a sentence to test your mic.
Hello, I'm here, and I'm glad to be here with Haskell Monroe.
Daniel, I want to introduce yourself to someone who may read the transcript or hear the tape.
Daniel, describe briefly your family background, from where, when, and what brought you to this community, and where to high school, and so forth.
My mom came originally from Mexico City.
My dad was born in Hondo, Texas, and there's a 20 years difference between my mom and dad.
My mom and dad...
...had met each other in Taylor, Texas.
My mom's brothers and dad had come to Texas in the early part of the century, trying to find their way into a better situation.
And they stayed here for about a year or two, then they sent for my mom and her mother and sisters, which has been the pattern for many, many years.
And they came, and my dad met them.
He was born in Texas, and by the time my dad met them, my dad had already been married three times, had three children from his prior marriage, and met my mom in Taylor, Texas.
And then they married and moved to Wheelock, Texas, which is in Robertson County.
And there, my mom and dad were involved in sharecropping.
They're at the Seal Farm.
And they were there for several years.
All along this time, there was a gentleman by the name of Pablo Hernandez, who was a friend of my dad's, an older gentleman, much older than my dad.
And he would always share the gospel with my dad about having a changed life, a new spiritual life.
And my dad said that he would...
He would humor him and go with him to the church functions because they had good cover dish dinners and suppers and lunches.
And so he would do that on Sundays.
And eventually something happened where it really caught hold of my dad spiritually, and it really transformed his life.
This is important because this was the pivotal point that sort of began to make him think differently about himself and the family.
As a result of this new way of thinking, a new thinking of himself,
of the family.
And by this time, I think there must have been about four children from my mom's...
that mom had had with him.
And he had his three other children, who by this time were already adults.
And they were often married.
But my dad decided to ask the owner of the land if he would drive my sister and drop her off at schoolhouse.
My sister Isabel.
And for some reason, the gentleman said no.
Or it couldn't happen, it wouldn't happen, whatever.
And so that really, really touched my dad.
It just changed...
He told me later that it was weird because had it been before he had had this transformed, new life, spiritual relationship with God,
it would have just been one of those other setbacks.
Plus, he said I probably wouldn't have even asked him.
But he said...
That night he went out to the cotton field and just sort of cried and just said, you know, there's got to be something better.
And so somehow he just felt like there was a need to move.
And so they, within a couple of weeks or so, picked up their stuff, made the decision and moved to Bryan, 20 miles away.
And so they came to Bryan.
And at that time, this is about in the 30s, late 30s, early 40s.
My dad, having developed a real spiritual reawakening, he started working but also started preaching, having church services at his house.
And so he would have church services and work at the Texas Highway Department and just little by little.
And since agriculture was also part of his background, he began to obtain contracts with some of the local farmers and landowners.
Landowners in the Brazos Bottom.
And would contract labor to work in the fields.
And that's what my brothers and I did all along for the most part of our younger life.
My oldest brother, my oldest two brothers, and my sister, Isabelle, or all of my brothers and I finished high school at Bryan High.
Which then used to be Stephen F. Austin in Bryan.
Mm-hmm.
My first sister, the first person in our family to finish school was my sister, Isabelle.
Because there was no college here or A&M did not accept women, this was back in the early 50s, she went to Baldwin-McKinsey College.
McKinsey-Bowen College, a business college here, a year program or something for secretarial training.
And that's where my sister went.
My older brothers, my older brother, Mike, finished high school probably about 15 years ago.
Sometime around that time, 56.
And he was 21 years old when he finished high school.
And my brother, Andrew, who finished about a year later, two years later, was also 21.
And the reason they ended up being 21 when he finished was because my dad would take them out from school and they would work the fields.
During this interim, what they would end up doing is they would work the fields here and also go to Lubbock, Texas and work.
And invariably sometimes there was a six months missed or a year missed, but they did miss a lot of school, so they were older students.
And my brother, Mike, was the first one to start Texas A&M.
And I would say that was probably like 1956, 57.
And starting the Corps of Cadets.
Right, and so you finished high school what year?
I finished high school in 1969.
Five brothers later.
I was the last of ten children.
And there were six boys and four girls.
One of my sisters died when she was like two years old.
And so my brother, Mike, started going to college right after high school.
And at first it was a real struggle for my dad, though he wanted him to go to educated.
But, you know, the reality started.
He said in that there was less time for them to work with him and work the fields.
Fortunately for him, he had a lot of other sons coming in along the line.
So every two years there was a new child and it was six boys.
So fortunately there was one of us becoming of age to be able to drive or work.
So that made it easier.
But my dad had to find a new definition of what I guess one would call a man.
Because in his mind, a man was someone who worked with his hands.
Was sweating.
Was always out in the fields.
And now his sons, who he admired a lot, were now in a clean shirt, no sweat.
It was working and trying to go to college.
Even though while they were going to college they did work at the international shoe factory here.
But it was a different kind.
And I think at first my dad had, it was tough for him because it was this whole notion that,
they were becoming not that rugged person that he was accustomed to them being.
Even though they had lived through that for the most of their lives.
At about what age did you become aware that college would be expected of you?
I would say when I was probably about eight or nine years old.
And it's interesting because we would still work in the cotton fields.
We would go to the migrant workers.
We would go into Lubbock and Brownfield, Texas.
We would leave like in late May, spend all through August chopping cotton.
And my dad would take the whole family with us.
And then we would come back for about a month.
And then late September we would go back to the same farm and then pick the cotton.
And I remember when I was probably about nine years old, I began to sign my name,
Daniel Hernandez, class of 73.
Now that, I remember the teachers thought of thinking that funny.
But my brothers were going to school.
And I remember telling my, the teachers in Lockville, excuse me,
I forgot the name of the school where I went to.
But we would tell them.
I would tell them I have brothers that were Aggies.
And of course it was incongruent because she knew we were farm workers.
But I think one time I came with an Aggie t-shirt and might have.
Right.
Now you left here before the spring semester was over to go work.
Well, normally we were right after school.
Right after school.
All right.
And then in the fall you'd be picking cotton.
Right.
And would you all just be absent from work?
Were you absent from the Bryan schools?
We were absent from the Bryan schools.
And my dad would enroll us in Lubbock in the schools,
but sometimes he would keep us about two or three weeks when we first got there
because he needed us to get him started.
And so then we'd go for about a month, a month and a half,
and then he'd pull us out about two or three weeks before the semester was over.
Okay.
So we ended up going to school about six weeks.
And at the front end, missing three weeks.
At the back end, another three weeks.
And so none of us ever failed.
We all made the grade and made good grades over there and made good grades here.
And my brother Mike, who was the first one to go through this whole process,
was the one that really just drove it into us.
You've got to study.
We couldn't.
My mom was very, very faithful to the Bible and would not let us read funny books.
So at home we could not read funny books.
We either had to be reading the Bible.
We were one of the first to get a television here in Bryan,
and I guess in 1956 we must have gotten the first TV.
But we could not watch TV during the week.
My brother would not let us watch TV during the week because we had to
and we couldn't read funny books.
So we left the Bible.
And so on weekends we could watch TV.
When I became a sophomore in high school,
my older brother then, who was now at home,
because every time one would leave, another brother would take over,
my brother David would let us watch one hour of TV a night.
Now, did those brothers assume a fatherly role?
Were they the male rule enforcer?
My dad was the ultimate enforcer.
But if it ever got to him, it's because things were pretty bad.
And so we never wanted to have things get to my dad.
But the way my dad reared us was that if your older brother is there, he's in charge.
And so my dad was working a lot, and so that was the way it was.
If my brother that was two years older than me was there and I was 10 and he was 12,
he was the guy that I had to obey.
What might have been the punishment if you had broken some of those rules?
Probably a good whacking in the bottom.
Though I'll have to admit, my dad was very lenient on me,
and he kind of did and spanked me,
and my other brothers weren't too crazy about that.
But my brothers were the ones that put the fear in me.
Because my dad may not have spanked me,
but my brother Mike always had a belt
around his chair, and he said,
You see this belt right here?
Do you want some of that belt?
And of course I said no.
And every once in a while I might cry mercy for my dad,
but my brothers were pretty much the ones that were the disciplinarians.
So now you graduate high school what year?
And do you remember what part of the year you applied for admission to A&M?
No, it was probably in the spring.
I was pretty nervous because I was the last one to have started A&M,
and I got admitted.
And I was nervous when I started in September
because I kind of felt a burden on me because I thought,
Here all my brothers have gone.
By that time I think two or three had already graduated,
and the others were still in school.
And it was like, Am I going to make it?
And of course it was more of a,
I wanted to make sure that I did as good as they had.
Now your senior class at Stephen F. Austin, about how many?
400 students.
Okay.
And was there a large number of them going to A&M?
You know, I don't know how many.
I know a lot of my classmates did.
Percentage-wise I couldn't tell you.
But you had a number of acquaintances.
Oh yes, I had a number of acquaintances.
All right.
Did you have one or two close buddies that were going to A&M?
Yeah.
I had two or three friends of mine that I had played ball with,
David Scasta and Ronald Spees.
These were guys that I had gone to elementary school with,
junior high and high school, and pretty bright guys,
and they ended up coming to A&M also.
All right.
Can you describe now the routine of registering
and going to your first classes, the first year?
Well, I remember registering was just pretty straightforward,
and I remember getting a letter of admission,
and I guess by the time I was going to school,
for me to go to A&M was important,
but it was not one of those things that people jumped up and down,
because it was expected of me.
I was just doing the next thing in line,
and so not that it lost its flavor,
it was just it was expected.
I guess my first days of class,
I remember not feeling nervous,
but just feeling excited about going to school.
I did see some of my friends in some of our bigger classes,
like history and English.
Where was registration held then,
Sebesa or G. Raleigh?
Hmm.
Because wasn't this still the time when you stood in line
and went from counter to counter?
Yeah, you had to go to counter to counter.
I don't remember, quite frankly.
You don't remember any unusual?
No.
Okay.
No, it was pretty straightforward.
Now, you lived at home.
I lived at home as my brothers had before me.
Did you encounter any kind of experiences that first fall,
being a minority student,
or anything else?
No.
No, I didn't.
I think because, I mean, I had played around on campus.
I had watched Randy Madsen set the world record.
I rode my bike over here.
So I was like at home.
What I think I found that was kind of unusual
is some of the students were not used to seeing
a lot of Hispanics going to school,
and I think for them it was kind of,
it was just a difference.
No one ever said negative, derogatory things,
but there was a sense of disconnectedness
with some of the students.
But as far as a campus or feeling a part of the campus,
that was something I always felt a part of the campus,
and it was always, you know, as far as I was concerned,
you know, that was my campus.
I'd been there since I was a kid.
Now, you have always struck me as being a person
I would call a mainstreamer,
that instead of being on the outside,
you've always tried to be on the inside.
Was that deliberate with you?
Did you think about that, or was that just normal?
You mean regarding just the political concepts
or ideas related?
Well, that was just part of my family upbringing.
I think having been a part of the United Methodist Church
taught us a lot.
Taught us a lot of leadership skills.
All my brothers and I were leaders in church conferences,
which included several hundred students and youth,
and we were always doing public speaking.
We were always in the middle,
always expected to be in the middle.
And so there was an expectation among the Hernandez boys
and girls to be in the middle and to participate.
So when coming here, I ended up pursuing
some of the same kind of activities.
Can you share any recollections
of that first semester of classes?
What did you take?
From whom?
Any experiences?
Oh, I remember taking a class from my freshman math class
from, you may have to help me here,
he's at A&M right now as their assistant provost.
Bill Perry?
No, the guy who handles all the fiscal affairs.
I forget his name.
He was my math professor in Algebra 102.
And then they threw in all of the engineers
along with all the social science and liberal arts.
And of course I was there, I was doing well,
because I had done well in high school,
but I was with a bunch of engineers
who could just eat that math up.
And that was, you know, that was the first time
I really was facing the idea of making a C in math.
And it kind of like shocked me,
because it wasn't that I didn't understand it,
it was just the material was going faster
than I had been used to,
because these kids were picking it up a lot faster.
I had my English teacher,
I remember this quite vividly and I forget her name,
but it was the year that Midnight Cowboy came out.
And the first English paper, my brothers had told me,
the first paper you write,
you're going to probably make an F on it,
because they're going to write it up
and you're going to just, like, you don't know how to write.
Well, that's what happened.
I mean, I wrote it, I think I made a D,
barely made a D.
And then my teacher talked to me,
and so we were supposed to write a narrative on the movie,
a movie we saw, or some book.
So I did it on the movie Midnight Cowboy.
And after all the critique that she had given me,
she came back, and I loved it, the movie was great,
and I came back with an A.
And, I mean, that just gave me the confidence that I needed
and it made me feel great.
And so it really helped me pick up a lot of my feeling about,
feeling good about myself
and that I was going to do well in school.
My history professor,
Alan Ashcraft.
Alan Ashcraft?
Alan Ashcraft was my history professor.
And he was funny, he was joyful,
he was a delight to listen to,
and he really made history come alive.
Did he have a nickname?
You know how students gave it, everybody?
No.
I didn't know of one if he had one.
No, no, no.
But he had a very interesting mannerism about how he talked.
He was a big fella,
but he had a very soft way of talking and,
no, but he, of course,
in those days using the word damn and hell
was not very common in public schools,
but when he used it,
it just made the class,
the word came in at the right time.
You took, do you remember how many hours you took that first semester?
First semester I think I took 13 hours.
And you did okay in terms of?
Yeah.
I think I made like a,
I came out with a,
I can't remember.
Did you, you weren't in the Corps.
No.
Did you get involved in any Aggie activities that fall?
That fall semester I did not because I was working.
What I would do,
I'd have my classes Mondays,
Wednesdays, Fridays in the morning,
and then at 2 o'clock in the afternoon,
I would work at Alinko.
So, and I would work from,
from 2 to 10 in the evening,
and that was my routine,
so.
And then what did you do on Tuesday,
Thursday?
Tuesday and Thursday I'd study.
Oh, okay.
And I'd go work,
but I would study,
but.
So you were working three eight-hour days,
or three eight-hour shifts,
Monday, Wednesday,
Friday?
Monday,
Wednesday, Friday,
but also I went on Tuesdays and Thursdays to work.
Oh, oh,
so you worked a full,
a full week.
Yeah, a full time was because my oldest brother,
Mike, when he was like 25,
he and Andrew decided to help my parents,
and they purchased a home for my parents.
And the way it worked,
it was that the boys and the girls,
we would all pay for the house.
So, as we became of age at 18,
we would all pick up the slack.
So it just worked out beautifully,
because we were responsible for paying the mortgage
and the utilities for a house.
And then, of course,
anything else, we bought it,
if we bought our car,
which I decided to buy me a car later.
All of that was up on us,
so we had to be working in order to meet the obligation of the house and utilities,
which nowadays is hardly anything,
but in those days it was,
it was enough.
Now, do you remember what your A&M bill was?
Do you remember what a semester cost?
I'm thinking something like,
hmm, three or four hundred dollars.
I mean, I really don't remember.
And you paid that yourself?
Yes.
Yes.
And how did you get to the campus that first semester?
The first semester,
I ended up,
my dad had a,
oh no, I ended up going with my brothers.
I ended up going with my brothers,
because we were going to class,
we all had to,
my other brother Moises had to also meet,
we worked together at the same place.
So we would set up our classes,
Monday, Wednesday,
Fridays, in the morning,
and then we would drive.
The year later,
the summer, the summer of 1970 is when I bought me a,
my own vehicle,
a used vehicle,
and then from there I had my own schedule.
And what advice did those older brothers give you about being a college student?
Well, you know,
it's interesting because the advice was just something that had been given all the time.
It's almost like,
it was expected,
and they would ask how they're going.
And, but my oldest brothers by that time were married and gone.
They were living in Houston.
And so at home,
it was only those of us that were still in school.
Because every time one of the brothers graduated,
they would move out.
My oldest sister,
my oldest sister lived in Bryan.
She was married.
And my other sister had moved to California.
And my youngest sister,
she worked here on campus,
but later on finished her degree at A&M.
So she was the only sister that finished Texas A&M.
But they didn't tell you about,
don't take this course or use?
No, no,
it was pretty much,
you know, I guess we didn't talk much about what,
what to expect or not expect,
because we pretty much knew that it was,
just get in there and do it.
All right.
Did you know,
were your rules of conduct and behavior as a college student?
Because of your family and?
Well, I guess I,
I always respected A&M.
I respected the traditions.
Those were just,
I mean, I grew up not walking on the grass.
I grew up not putting a cap on.
I grew up,
you know, singing the Aggie War Hymn,
the Spirit of Aggieland.
So all those things were just a part of me.
To me, it was just like another extension of,
of what it was to be a person,
a human being,
an American.
I mean,
and so, in that way,
it was quite unique.
I think respecting my elders,
I mean, all that was very much an expectation.
We'd see some students who were perhaps on the,
on the fringe of the hippie,
but really, but as hippies they got was that they got,
they had long hair,
but it wasn't,
I think, to the extreme that you would see in either campus across the state.
All right.
But then in your private life,
what were the rules that you knew you were to follow?
Well, I pretty much had to,
this being my home and my parents being Christian,
my parents, I never,
this may sound strange,
but drinking was never one of those things that I had ever had aspired to,
or it was just not one of those things,
or smoking, or,
or doing drugs.
It was just never one of those things that I never thought about doing in a serious way.
And, and I guess what helped a lot was that the people I hung around with,
neither did they.
What about what hours of the night you were to be home?
Well, my dad,
once we were 18,
pretty much it was really up to us.
But during the day,
during the week hours,
we were working.
So we'd get home about 10,
And we were,
maybe study a little while,
and eat, and then go to bed,
and get up the next day.
They would be,
they would be,
my dad, I hardly ever saw him except on,
on Saturdays and Sundays.
Because we were.
Yeah.
He was in completely different schedules.
So I didn't see him.
He would get up early in the morning and go to work.
And then I was working when he got back.
And so my mom was the one that was,
who would give us breakfast and then leave us something to eat at night.
But she was the one that we would always talk with.
All right.
That first fall,
you said you went to Yale practice.
Did you go to any silver taps?
Yes.
Yes.
Went to silver taps.
What was your impression at your first silver taps?
I guess it was pretty,
I guess I had never seen something like that.
I related that to,
wow, this is some solemn stuff that we do at church.
Or something that,
you know, this is,
I guess what struck me was that it was students who were,
who were engaged in this very solemn ritual that struck me,
you know, regardless of what you were doing 30 minutes before,
you know, when it came,
everybody's quiet in a very meditative,
you know, meditating kind of a mood.
And I think that's what began to connect the dots for me about,
A&M and its traditions.
You know, you begin to,
you go through the traditions and you see them and you hear them as a young kid.
But when you begin to see these things all sort of fall in line,
they all make a lot of sense.
Did you go to fish camp?
No.
I don't even know if they had fish camp in the 19th.
They didn't.
Yeah,
they didn't.
Cause I used to go up there to speak about what land grant education was.
I was not one of them.
Did you have anything to do with bonfire?
No, I didn't because like I said,
I was working.
Did you, did you attend bonfire?
Oh yes.
In fact,
my, my wife,
my date, my first date with my wife was at Aggie Bonfire.
Was it that first year?
No, no,
no, no.
It was in 1972.
I was a senior and,
but I, I never could work at bonfire because we were working those,
those hours, which.
Now wait, I thought you said you began.
When did you enroll?
At AM?
Oh,
okay.
Yeah.
All right.
Well,
let's jump forward to that.
You mentioned that date.
Describe that, that bonfire with that date.
Oh, well,
I was, I guess I was trying to impress her that I was a student and she was impressed,
I guess.
And we went to the bonfire and she had never been to anything like that.
She was from up in Lubbock and never had,
she had gone to West Texas State University.
There was nothing like that at West Texas State.
And this was a big deal.
I mean, and I think for her being in a place where there were mainly whites,
all whites, and for,
for us to participate in,
in the school life and participate in what's going on was very,
was very different because that was not what she was accustomed to.
So,
